title: Zarhin's trick and geometric boundedness results for K3 surfaces
speaker: François Charles
abstract: Tate's conjecture for divisors on algebraic varieties can be rephrased as a finiteness statement for certain families of polarized varieties with unbounded degrees. In the case of abelian varieties, the geometric part of these finiteness statements is contained in Zarhin's trick. We will discuss such geometric boundedness statements for K3 surfaces over arbitrary fields and holomorphic symplectic varieties, with application to direct proofs of the Tate conjecture for K3 surfaces that do not involve the Kuga-Satake correspondence.

proof_stages.label: Tate divisors as Brauer-group finiteness
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the cycle class map, restricts to divisors, compares with the complex Lefschetz (1,1) theorem, and uses the Kummer sequence to reformulate the Tate conjecture for divisors as finiteness of the relevant primary torsion in the Brauer group.

proof_stages.label: Abelian-variety Zarhin prototype
proof_stages.summary: Tate's theorem on endomorphisms of abelian varieties over finite fields is sketched via Galois-invariant subspaces, finite-index approximations by isogenies, and Zarhin's boundedness trick that makes the approximating abelian varieties take only finitely many values.

proof_stages.label: K3 boundedness question and Tate link
proof_stages.summary: After defining K3 surfaces and recalling that they are unbounded without fixing degree but have moduli at fixed degree, the speaker explains that for K3 surfaces over finite fields the Tate conjecture is tied to finiteness of isomorphism classes, with Brauer classes producing twisted-sheaf moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Mukai moduli-space construction
proof_stages.summary: The Mukai lattice and Mukai vector are introduced, and results of Mukai, O'Grady, and Yoshioka identify suitable moduli spaces of stable sheaves on a K3 surface as smooth projective varieties deformation equivalent to Hilbert schemes, with second cohomology given by the orthogonal complement of the Mukai vector.

proof_stages.label: Lattice-theoretic K3 Zarhin trick
proof_stages.summary: By adding a hyperbolic plane and choosing a small positive rank-two lattice containing the Mukai vector and a divisor class, the argument transfers large polarization data into negative directions, produces a bounded positive Beauville--Bogomolov square on the moduli space, and retains enough discriminant or transcendental-lattice control to recover boundedness for the original K3 surface.

proof_stages.label: Big line bundles and birational boundedness
proof_stages.summary: Huybrechts's theorem gives bigness from positive Beauville--Bogomolov square, and over the complex numbers a proposition shows that after moving within the big cone a bounded multiple gives a birational map to projective space with bounded degree, using period-domain boundedness rather than an effective Matsusaka theorem.

proof_stages.label: Conclusion: Tate via geometric boundedness
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes that the K3 analogue of Zarhin's trick gives an essentially geometric and birational-boundedness route toward the Tate conjecture for K3 surfaces, with the characteristic-p issues reduced to boundedness and birational geometry.

examples.name: Complex divisor case via Lefschetz (1,1)
examples.note: The exponential sequence proof of the Lefschetz (1,1) theorem is used as a comparison showing how a divisor-cycle statement can rest on a finiteness theorem for coherent cohomology.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly runs the exponential exact sequence for complex divisors, identifies the boundary map Pic to H2, and relates its kernel/image to Lefschetz (1,1) and GAGA.

examples.name: Elliptic surface Brauer torsors
examples.note: For an elliptic surface, Brauer classes correspond to torsors under the Jacobian fibration, illustrating how Brauer finiteness becomes boundedness of a family whose polarizations appear unbounded geometrically.
examples.engagement_reason: elliptic surfaces are invoked as the motivating Brauer-torsor picture, with facts stated about torsors and polarizations but no concrete torsor construction or calculation carried out.
